7 min read
Just now

--

Press enter or click to view image in full size

A few years ago, if someone asked me where they should start learning System Design, my answer was almost automatic. I would recommend Educative’s Grokking the System Design Interview. At the time, it felt like one of the few resources that transformed a vague, intimidating interview topic into a structured learning journey that engineers could actually follow.
Fast forward to 2026, and the landscape looks completely different.
There are now dozens of System Design courses, YouTube channels, newsletters, AI tutors, and interview platforms competing for attention. Every few months another creator claims they’ve built the definitive System Design course or that the original Grokking course has finally been surpassed. After hearing that argument enough times, I decided to revisit the course myself.
I expected it to feel dated.
Instead, I found myself appreciating it even more than I remembered.
That doesn’t mean it’s perfect, and it certainly shouldn’t be the only resource you use. But after spending time comparing it with many of today’s alternatives, I still think it’s one of the best places to build a solid System Design foundation.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Why I Decided to Revisit It
I’ve spent more than eight years working on backend systems, APIs, distributed services, and cloud infrastructure. During that time, I’ve also helped engineers prepare for technical interviews, and one pattern has remained remarkably consistent.
Most people don’t struggle because they’ve never heard of Redis, Kafka, or load balancers. They struggle because they don’t know how to combine those technologies into a coherent design while explaining why each decision makes sense.
That’s what makes System Design difficult.
It’s not about memorizing architecture diagrams. It’s about developing a structured way of thinking through open-ended problems.
With so many newer courses now available, I wanted to see whether the original Grokking course still taught that engineering mindset as well as I remembered.
The Course Still Focuses on the Right Things
One thing that immediately stood out while revisiting the course is how well it stays focused on fundamentals instead of trends.
Technology changes incredibly quickly. Every year there are new databases, cloud services, AI frameworks, and architectural patterns competing for attention. It’s tempting for educational platforms to constantly chase those trends, but the best engineering education usually lasts because it teaches principles rather than products.
That’s exactly what Grokking continues to do.
Rather than teaching a particular cloud provider or framework, it focuses on concepts that remain relevant regardless of the technology stack you’re using. Topics like scalability, load balancing, caching, database partitioning, replication, messaging systems, fault tolerance, and capacity estimation are still the building blocks of modern distributed systems, and they’re explained in a logical progression that makes each new concept easier to understand than the last.
It Teaches You How to Think
One thing I appreciated much more this time around is that the course spends very little time encouraging memorization.
Many modern System Design resources immediately jump into questions like “Design Instagram” or “Design YouTube.” Those case studies are useful, but they often begin with a finished architecture instead of explaining how an experienced engineer arrived at that solution.
The original Grokking course approaches the problem differently.
It teaches you to begin with requirements, estimate scale, identify bottlenecks, discuss trade-offs, and gradually evolve the design as new constraints appear. That process closely mirrors how real System Design interviews unfold and, more importantly, how real engineering decisions are made.
By the time you reach the larger design exercises, you’re no longer copying architecture diagrams. You’re building them yourself, one decision at a time.
The Advanced Topics Still Feel Relevant
One criticism I occasionally hear is that because the course has been around for several years, it must be missing many of the concepts that appear in modern interviews.
After revisiting it, I don’t think that’s a fair criticism.
The technologies surrounding distributed systems continue to evolve, but the engineering challenges remain remarkably similar. Applications still need to scale, databases still need to replicate data, services still communicate through asynchronous messaging, and engineers still need to balance consistency, availability, latency, and cost.
The course covers a wide range of topics that continue to appear in interviews and production systems, including caching strategies, load balancing, database replication, partitioning, message queues, search architecture, API design, high availability, disaster recovery, and capacity estimation. Those are concepts that backend engineers encounter throughout their careers.
I Actually Like the Interactive Format
One thing that’s often overlooked when people compare System Design courses is how they teach.
Many newer platforms rely almost entirely on video. There’s nothing wrong with that, but I’ve found that passive watching doesn’t always translate into deeper understanding. It’s surprisingly easy to finish a two-hour video feeling productive without actually retaining much of what you’ve learned.
Educative takes a different approach.
The interactive lessons encourage you to read, think, answer questions, and actively work through the material instead of simply consuming it. Personally, I retain much more information that way because I’m forced to slow down and process each concept before moving on.
I understand that some people strongly prefer video-based learning, and if that’s you, another platform may be a better fit. But for my own learning style, the interactive format remains one of the course’s biggest strengths.
I Still Supplemented It With Other Resources
Like most experienced engineers, I rarely rely on a single resource when I’m learning something new. A structured course gives me the roadmap, but I usually reinforce what I’ve learned by reading guides, exploring different explanations, and studying how experienced engineers approach similar problems. I found that combining multiple perspectives helped the concepts stick much better than relying on one course alone.
If you’re working through Grokking the System Design Interview, I’d also recommend exploring these free resources:

• Complete Guide to System Design — It’s an excellent companion if you want another perspective on the core concepts.

• Advanced System Design for Principal Engineers — A deeper dive into large-scale architecture.

• System Design Interview Guide — I found it useful whenever I wanted to explore a topic beyond what was covered in the course.

• Grokking the System Design Interview — A free, detailed course.
I don’t think any single course can teach everything there is to know about System Design. What worked best for me was using Grokking as the foundation, then reinforcing those concepts through additional reading, real-world case studies, and plenty of practice designing systems on my own.
Who I Think This Course Is Best For
If you’re preparing for your first System Design interviews, I still think this is one of the strongest places to begin. The structured progression removes much of the uncertainty that often discourages people from learning System Design in the first place, and the interview framework is something you’ll continue using long after the course is finished.
It’s also an excellent choice for backend engineers who understand software development but haven’t yet designed systems at massive scale. You don’t need years of distributed systems experience to understand why these architectural patterns exist, and the course does a good job of bridging that gap.
If you’re already operating at a Staff or Principal Engineer level, however, you’ll probably want additional resources covering advanced distributed systems, production architecture, and organizational decision-making. That’s less a limitation of the course and more a reflection of its intended audience.
My Biggest Takeaway
Revisiting this course reminded me of something I think we often forget in technology.
Good engineering education ages much more slowly than technology itself.
Cloud platforms change. AI transforms how we build software. New databases appear every year. But the fundamental trade-offs behind distributed systems remain remarkably consistent. We still need to think about scalability, resilience, latency, consistency, reliability, and cost regardless of which technologies happen to be popular.
That’s why I think the original Grokking course continues to hold up so well. It teaches you how to reason about systems instead of simply introducing another collection of tools.
In my experience, that’s a much more valuable skill.
Final Thoughts
So, after revisiting Educative’s original Grokking the System Design Interview in 2026, would I still recommend it?
Absolutely.
Not because it’s the newest course or the most heavily marketed one, but because it continues to do something many resources still struggle with: it teaches engineers how to think about System Design.
If I were building a learning roadmap today, I’d still start here. I’d work through the course carefully, reinforce the concepts with additional reading and real-world case studies, and spend plenty of time designing systems on my own. I’d also use AI tools to ask follow-up questions whenever I wanted another explanation or a different perspective on a difficult topic.
No single course will make you an expert in distributed systems, and no course can replace the lessons you learn from building software in production. But if you’re looking for a structured, thoughtful, and still remarkably relevant introduction to System Design, I think Educative’s original Grokking course remains one of the best investments you can make.
